speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape nine. We've more or less come up to date. You've had-- you've told me an amazing story-- in tremendous detail, with graphic detail, with tremendous-- with dreadful tragedies and things, and yet you talk about it in an amazingly calm way. Do you know how does that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, partly I suppose because, well, a Holocaust experience is partly because-- because I worked at the Wiener Library for four and a half years, which was a library of the Holocaust. And I was constantly confronted with the Holocaust material and also with questions constantly, because I was the only person there who was a camp survivor. And there was the Eichmann trial on, and there were other incidents coming on, and newspaper people came and reporters came, and-- and people who worked there said, ask her. She knows about it. She was there. I didn't--I must say, I was so badly paid that I didn't particularly like to be on top of it constantly, be stirred up. And-- but probably that's why I-- because I always spoke about it. I really always spoke right from the beginning. But besides that, because I always kept my sense of proportion, my sense of humor-- because I always faced up to things, didn't try to put them under the-- sweep them under the carpet. And I always took looks things as they came, and dealt with them as they came. And-- well, otherwise I couldn't have survived. And also because perhaps I kept my self-respect-- because except for that one incident, I-- I behaved like a human. I didn't-- felt, at the end of the war, that I won the war, because they couldn't reduce me-- my human dignities. They couldn't take away my human dignity. I kept my self-respect, and that was very important for me-- even more than-- even more than my integrity, even more than survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is that-- is it partly that that you put your survival down to, the fact that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My survival I put down to the fact that I was 20 years old, that we were-- that Hungary was taken so late in the war-- because I wouldn't have survived another week. I mean, I was really so weak at the time when I dropped, that I wouldn't have survived if it had been a bit longer. So that in that way I was extremely lucky. Some other people went through much more and had much longer periods and-- and had to-- had to put up with even more, lots of beatings, which I didn't have. I mean, it was very tough. It was no-- no holiday, but it was-- some other stories that I read, it was much worse still. So I, in a way, I was still lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time for you after the war was almost more difficult than the-- certainly because it was a longer period of time-- than perhaps your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the consequence is that I became-- that I didn't go back to Hungary. And because of that, I became stateless. And because I became stateless, I had all this-- all these difficulties in all the countries I lived, because I had to live illegally, work illegally. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you gone back to Hungary, you would have had a-- got a-- have got a passport, and things would have been completely-- it would've been different. Is that what you wonder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, had I gone back to Hungary, there would have been one thing-- I could have done a three-months refresher course, and my qualifications would have been recognized internationally. And I would have had no problems as a nursery school teacher, which I had here. For instance, when I came to England, my qualifications weren't recognized. In Israel, they weren't fully-- they wanted me, but they wanted me only as a dogsbody more in the nursery school, not run a nursery school-- not-- not really doing work that I wanted to do. So it's that my qualifications weren't recognized. They weren't valid. And-- and therefore, I-- but I never regretted not going back. I mean, it was my choice. And I didn't go back for a very, very long time. I went back in '70 to see my French teacher, and a family friend who survived and who went back and lived in the town, and who were both 80. And I knew that they wouldn't be living-- I had thought that they wouldn't be living very long anymore, and so I wanted to see them again. And I spent four hours in my hometown, and I was sick the whole night afterwards. And then, until quite a long time, I didn't go. And in the last few years we have been going to a spa, and I-- but mainly to Budapest. And last year, I was-- well, no, I was back in my hometown, invited back for the 50th anniversary of our school leaving-- which-- about which I spoke already. And then there was a 50th anniversary of our deportation-- which, the town put up an absolutely fantastic show. But it seemed a very sincere show. The young mayor said that-- it was a whole day's affair, and it was incredibly done. And the young mayor in the speech said that anybody-- to the Hungarians of that town, who were there, and there were very many, hundreds and hundreds there-- that anybody who allowed, permitted that their neighbors should be carried away and didn't open their mouth shouldn't consider themselves civilized human beings. And the three priests from the three Christian faiths who were there-- each of them said that it's high time that we admit our guilt in what has happened, and that we see that in our teaching, religion teaching, they don't do anything that can cause anti-Semitism. Which was a wonderful thing, too. And then I was asked back into my school to speak to my-- to the pupils there, which I did last year. And they brought in pupils from another school, as well. And I have done that in Hungarian. I was very proud that I still, after so many years I could still get up and speak in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what has happened to your Jewish faith, you know, through all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- faith, I haven't got any. I'm a non-believer. I got-- oddly enough, there was a time when I was a Hebrew teacher, when I was teaching-- when I was preparing children for bar mitzvah. My children, they took all beautiful bar mitzvah. They didn't learn very much-- they learned a lot of history. They learned, I must say, I taught some like a parrot-- the parsha, because anyway, most of them didn't understand very much what they were saying, and I don't believe in putting up children onto a-- onto a-- onto a podium--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the bimah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on a bimah to-- to say something like parrot, where they don't understand what they are doing. But I told them I couldn't care less if they make a mistake. And, therefore, they didn't, because there was no pressure on them. And we spent the time doing history and language, and practically each of them stayed after their bar mitzvah lessons-- stayed on either to do all their verse in Hebrew, or to go and just learn Jewish history.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure they had very inspired teaching from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew how not to teach, because I knew how-- what I-- how I have been taught.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there any-- is there any message that you want to sort of pass on from all you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you asked me about my belief. And my belief is do not-- do unto others what you don't want to be done to yourself-- [NON-ENGLISH]. But the message-- this is one of the messages-- if you don't want to be stolen from, you won't steal. But it-- the message is that people should, when I go to schools, my message usually is that they should evaluate. They shouldn't generalize. They shouldn't speak about that Jews, the Germans, the gypsies-- but they should look at individual people. They should look how would they like to be in the position. To evaluate what they are hearing from demagogues, and not accept everything, and not follow your leader without evaluating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, or before we bring your husband in on the tape, is there anything else that you'd like to say in conclusion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I-- I think I said it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trude, would you like to introduce Franz to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband, Franz Levi, and we have been together now, as I said, 26 years. And I am-- I am a very lucky person.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel the lucky person aside. I was born in Berlin. My parent-- my parents perished in the Holocaust. I was lucky enough to escape in 1936, just turning 16. I was lucky to go to Palestine at the time. A youth organization took me there. And lucky enough to go to a good school after this. This school gave me sufficient background, eventually, in life, to become a professional. It took a long, long, long time until I became an architect, and was scientific adviser to one of the large firms of architecture in this country, here. And it was the year 1990, I retired, at the age of 70. And started to become a historian. And I have the next 30 years to study and make my way in Jewish history. I've just-- in 1993, I published a publication in the Now, I'm on the verge of publishing an essay, a very large essay-- a book, in fact-- in Germany, in German, on the emancipation of Jews in Meiningen-- in Saxon-Meiningen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has it been being married to Trude in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was something totally new for me because, after all, her story is a story which is so difficult to bear, that I believed in the beginning it could impose on our marriage, on our life, but it hasn't. Trude is a very sane person. And none of her experience actually impinged on her life, or-- the fears had gone, the pain had gone partially. Of course, it will never disappear completely. But let me talk about some of the bits of evidence which I collected in the meantime. One of the pieces of evidence-- and may I bend down for a moment-- is contained in this file, here. For instance, there is a lovely piece of evidence that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Careful of Trude's face.

speaker: interviewee
text: That the SS considered the prisoners their property, and they sold their property to the factory in which Trude had to work as a slave worker. Here's an invoice. And the invoice was issued by the administration of the Russian SS, concentration camp, Buchenwald. Date, 6th of March, 1945. And 17,863 shifts are being sold for the princely amount of r reichsmark a shift. Makes, altogether 71,452 reichsmark-- and that the amounted, basically, to slave labor of round about 640 prisoners. I don't know exactly how they arrived at this figure. I presume there were a 1,000 Jewish prisoners in the beginning, which went to this factory. 206 were taken out in the selection, and the rest of them-- and that it comes about, if I deduct most probably absentees, or those which have disappeared in the meantime, those who died or suffered as a result of the slave labor-- you arrive at the 640 shifts which had to be done in the month of February, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's valuable evidence of what went on. Do you think I could talk some more? I think probably we should-- that it's-- it's Trude's tape and--

speaker: interviewee
text: You would like to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it's probably appropriate to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I like to discuss, in a few brief moments, our struggle to get a pension from the German government for the time in which Trude was a prisoner, or, let's call it a slave labor in Hessisch-Lichtenau. Hessisch-Lichtenau, as you may know by now, was an ammunition factory. And this ammunition factory exploited the work and, as I've shown before now, had to pay for the labor. And one would assume that payment for labor established a work relationship. This worker's relationship is being denied by the German government. It's being denied, actually, only for those who are not Americans, and those who are not Israelis. Because Israel and Americans-- and America has-- has established a contact with the German government to pay those victims of the time in slave labor. And, therefore, that those people are getting a pension. Not so in Britain. We have applied in 1989 for a pension for Trude. It took fully four years until the last day on the year 1993 for the German pension fund to respond to Trude, and denying her, with a letter, her being able to get a pension. In this pension statement they say, 'payment alone for work does not to confirm an employment relationship, requiring insurance. The exchange of work against payment was not on a voluntary base, but based on the special existence of a violent power situation, thereby excluding the duty for the contributory engagement relationship.' Now that's about-- here I see violence of the government, the way in which these people had been treated, is being held against them in order to refuse them a pension. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about the lady, the top photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, this was, I believe, the photograph of my parents when they got married in 1917, when my father was in the Austro-Hungarian army. And he was-- he was-- he had the highest decorations as a medical man-- that a medical man could get. And this is my mother with him. I believe I was about seven years old, or six years old when this one was taken. I love this photo. I think it's beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very pretty.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my maternal grandmother's 60th birthday celebration. We all went up to-- she lived in Vienna, and-- and she is in the center. On the right, there is-- well, I suppose it's the left of the picture, I don't know. On my right, is first-- my mother is sitting, and next to her, my brother stands. And then there is her sister. And then-- in-- then I am there, that little girl. My brother and I are rather frightened, because in those days the flash made a terrible noise, and they didn't warn us. And on the other side of my grandmother is her eldest son, Uncle Ernst, with his wife, Ilse, and with her son, Heinzi. And behind Ernst is the other brother, who is Otto. And then next to him is Frank, or Franz. Then there are the two younger men, who are sons of my mother's sister, who is between my brother and myself. And her husband, who stands behind her. And they are called George and Fridl. Fridl died a couple of years ago in Oregon-- no, perhaps a bit longer in Oregon. And George is still alive in-- in Australia-- Sydney, Australia. And I didn't mention my father, who is standing behind my brother and my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The picture was taken in 1927. When we arrived in Auschwitz on the first day, two SS women came and they started-- they asked our particulars. They took our particulars, and I suppose they gave us our number. Mine was 20,607. And this is the prisoner's personal card-- card. And the auditor in this one is, well, it says Mosonyi Getrud, and then Szombathely. And then it says that I'm from Hungary, and my father, and it mentions my mother, as well. And then the-- that Auschwitz, and that I was a [NON-ENGLISH], politic-- Hungarian-Jewish and political prisoner. The one odd thing on this one is, that the person who took it-- I said that I was a nursery school teacher when she asked my profession, and she-- and she took it as teacher. And then wrote it down, [GERMAN] with an umlaut, where-- she she couldn't even spell. And in German, it's much worse if a person can't spell, because it should have been with an E. Thank you. Now the other oddity is that there is-- it's printed there, the punishment in camp. And then underneath, they put my mother, Dora Ullmann, in Birkenau. So it seemed to be my punishment that my mother was gassed in Birkenau. Well, this is part of the list of the 206 who were taken away in that selection, where there were originally 208. And where there is a 01, that's where my name should be. And my number, 20,607, has been written over. And there is a note one next to it to show that that's-- that was a one-- two people were taken out, and there is a-- that was taken out. Well, I seem to have been a quite a happy little girl in 1930. And this was taken at my grandmother's-- in my grandmother's orchard, by the looks of it, in Zalaszentgrot. I don't know whether you want to spell it. This is my first husband. Well, this is my wedding to my first husband in 19-- in 8th of June, 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Paris. I-- well, I told everything about him. This is my son, Ilan's graduation in Oxford, when he did his DPhil, got his-- received his DPhil as a geneticist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a biologist, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I am afraid now I can't-- I have to-- would have to work it out. When he was 24. At '49--

speaker: interviewee
text: '49, '69-- '71. It would be '71, Well, this one would have been taken last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, Jonathon, who is 24 now. And this picture has been taken last year, in 1994. He is now doing his doctorate in Oxford-- in political science, political thought. He has got a PPE from Oxford, and then he has got a special diploma from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mannheim University for German. And then he has got an MA-- a two-years MA, which he did in French in Brussels in one year. And he's a very bright boy. This is Marina Deak, my French granddaughter, who lives with her mother and little brother and stepfather in Paris-- well, in Sevres, near Paris. And she is now 18. And this is her-- is in her second year at university doing, at the moment, philosophy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she's a very, very lovely girl. Well, I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the invoice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm? This is the invoice that Franz talked about before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, this is the invoice that France talked about before, thank you very much. I am at the moment blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: is part of the Holocaust series. It's published by Valentine-- it was published by Valentine Mitchell in London in-- this year, in 1995, January. And it mainly writes about my-- how I survived. And it-- it gives some background, as well. But I mainly wanted to show that if one survived camp, that still wasn't the end of it. And it is called because I met a cat in Germany that-- that was called Adolf, because it always raised its paw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how well is it doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it has sold 36 copies in the States-- and-- and nearly 2,000-- no, 1,200 in this country. And I hope it's going to do still well, and it has been translated into German now. And it's going to come out in Germany, but I don't know exactly-- I hope this coming year, it's coming out in Germany.
